speaker: interviewer
text: Tape, number seven, December, 21, 1995, Ira Kaminsky. And my name is Daniel Sedlis.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, coming to this Austria, to Linz, Austria, they ask me I should become an active member in a political organization Betar, Zionist organizations. And before the party was in existence, and as a matter of fact, most of the Jewish parties, political parties, were in existence when I came over there. And well, I said, I agreed. You know, if I'll see that it will fit for my-- for me. Willingly or unwillingly I became very active over there. And I became chairman of the organization over there in Linz. And then I was also a member of the central committee of our organization. And that time they organized also a Congress of the Jewish people in Austria. And I became, of course, a delegate. And I was talking over there. And we were organizing the people in Austria, the, the displaced people, the Jewish people in Austria. And people started to come in more involved. But, but what we didn't expect-- let me first, I became very active. And I became also a member of the Federation. I became Vice President of the Federation of the Zionist organizations in Austria. And we were-- I had to go and visit the various camps, this-- the DP camps, they called them, and organize people who were willing to go illegally with Aliyah Bet to Palestine that time. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Well, this was a very rewarding job that I had, and very prestigious job that I had at time over there. Then after a while, I decided that it's not just that we have to work for somebody to go to Israel. I decided with my sister and her boyfriend that we will go with the illegal, this organization, we'll go to Palestine. And we organized. It was from all the political parties, about 600 people. We had to go through France with the trains, and we're going to France. I was asking all the papers. They said it's OK. We went, 600 people. When we came to the border at the French zone over there in Austria, we had to cross-- we had to go through France. The police stopped us, checking the papers. And for some reason they didn't like the papers. Papers were officially to another country. And they said you're not going to go there. You're not going to cross through France. You have to go back to where you came from, everybody. I said, we don't go back. We have no place already. We lost our place. Wherever we were, we can't go back. The only place we can go is forward, but not back. Anyway, you'll have to go. And it started, you know, and arguing. You have to go. I said, we're not going to go. So it started a fight. They tried to put us back to the train, you know, to the-- and we, we, we didn't want to go back. I put in a stiff fight. My little brave sister was in front of me. And I was the last one to fight them back, the police. And we said that we're not going to go in. They called in 300 additional policemen, French policeman. And they wanted to force us, to take us in one by one. But we fought them. And when they saw that they can't do nothing with me, they hit me with the back of the rifle. And they hit me on my head. And I was very-- started bleeding very badly. And they-- and I was wounded. Then after that, they put us in-- but one thing they learned. We're not going to go back. They didn't send us back. Otherwise you're going to have a fight every single day, everyone will fight back. We're not going back. Anyway, they clo-- they took us to a certain closed-in camp, like in a prison. We were about two months over there, and very little food also. That's the way the French behaved. And finally they found out where we are, and the Jewish papers, they knew. They heard. They reported what we had, you know, the fight with the French police. And they let us off that closed-in camps. They brought us to Germany to Koblenz. In Koblenz, we were in a open camp. We could go. We were free people. But we didn't like to be there. Our aim was not to stay not in Germany, not in anywhere else, not in Austria, but to go to Palestine that time. Anyway, we got a word that we have to return to the American zone. And Germany over there, they're organizing a, a few hundred people to go with the-- to go to Palestine. We went. And we had good papers. And they brought us to France legally, to France. And then on a boat. And we went with the Ben Hecht boat. Ben Hecht was a writer here in the United States, on his name. He was a very active man for the-- for the Jew-- for the Jewish underground in Israel. And they--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know him? Did you know him?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't know him personally, no. And he was-- he was the sponsor for that. And, and we went to on our way from Marseille to Palestine. When we came close to Palestine, we noticed the airplanes, English airplanes surrounded us already. The started to fly over us. And right away some ships came. And they surrounded our ship. And they notified us that we should go, to follow them, their instructions. They took us to Haifa. But in Haifa, we didn't go down to Haifa. They had special, their own ship. And they were taking us to Cyprus. They had over there from before camps, closed camps, concentration camps that they kept. The illegal immigration to Palestine, they kept over there in Cyprus. In Cyprus, it was just like a German concentration camp, outside surrounded. And we were watched with the watchtowers and everything else. But it was different than with the Nazis. We didn't have enough food, it's true. Also but we knew that nobody is going to kill us. And we didn't expect that from the English people. But the only thing we were not allowed to go to Israel, to that time Palestine. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: And here you were with the brother and sister.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. My brother stayed in Linz. He didn't go with us. My sister went with me and her boyfriend at that time went with me. Anyway, we were sent to Cyprus through Lager 66, or whatever, some more thousands of more Jewish DPs, displaced people, were over there. And what we-- I asked them. They were ready for some time. So be prepared. Because they notified us only 1,500 people per year will leave this-- these camps.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I said, well, we're going to be here for what? For 10 years or something? No. Anyway. We were over there. And I became also very active, and a member of the central committee of our party. And if we have to stay there in the camp and do nothing, I consider it's very dangerous. If young people that do nothing, it's very-- it's not-- it's unproper to stay there. And, I had a job as an org-- organizer. [NON-ENGLISH], I had. I should organize the people. So we decided that we will organize. I decided schools. We-- we organized. We were looking for teachers, for Hebrew teachers mostly we needed. People didn't know Hebrew. We're going to Israel, at that time Palestine. They have to know the language, the history or geography-- all kind of various schools with teachers. We didn't have nothing else to do, you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we went to schools, and everybody, our party without any help from Israel because all the other parties had instructors sent from Israel. We were considered the outer party that was not with the administration of Israel, with Ben-Gurion Administration. And we organized the schools. And we did very, very well, and people learned until it came our turn. And this was-- and this was from after being over there 13, 14 months, we were sent to Palestine. We came March 20, to Palestine.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was 1948. We came March 20. We came to Palestine. So as you know, I had a sister over there. They took us to one place. And I didn't want to stay over there. I said, I've got a sister. And she's ready to receive me and my sister. My sister that she is already a long time, from 1932, in Palestine. And she's expecting us. We went over there. And I stayed with my sister, [INAUDIBLE]. And my Hannah, she was very happy to see us. She thought in the beginning that we didn't survive. And we-- I stayed over there in her house for a while. Then I moved out. And I started to work and support myself. I started plumbing. And, and I made a living, you know. And at that time-- that time right after I came, two weeks after I joined the Jewish underground there, the Irgun. And after a month, about two months, I joined them. It was in April. And June, the beginning of June, we had to join the Jewish [NON-ENGLISH], the Jewish army. In June, I joined the army and all the members from Irgun joined the army at that time. And I was, well, that time we all-- it was the War of Independence. And I was in the army also close to a year and a half. And after the army, I started to work, like I said before, plumbing. And I supported myself. It was very, very rough times.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do the army?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the army, I was in the-- in the technician's army. And, and my brother started to write to me and send some packages also, and help me. Because food was over there, even after the creation of the Jewish state, was very, very rough to get at that time. We had the wars, one after the other at that time. It started the war. Then it stopped. And he once, he asked me to come-- not to come-- to go to the United States. But he knew that I wouldn't go because I liked Israel, you know? It's the first time that I was a free man when I came to Israel. And I joined the army. I really considered myself as a free man, and I wanted-- and I could, you know, contribute something to the creation of the Jewish state. And he asked me if I would come for a visit to the United States. I said, yes. I would go because I had a large family here. My father had five brothers and sisters here in the United States. And I had many first cousins in the United States that I never met them. And they never met me either. And at that time-- and I agreed. And it took me a long time until I got permission to come to the United States, not like today. And, and in 19-- in the beginning of 1954, I came to United States. My brother was very happy. He felt-- he felt lonely in a sense. He got married.

speaker: interviewer
text: You came here to visit him?

speaker: interviewee
text: I came to visit my brother just for three months. I came to visit, and I was planning to go back. But my brother and my cousins had different plans. They said, we-- Israel-- they called me Israel, of course-- is single. If we will find him a girl, then he wouldn't go back. They wanted very badly I should stay here with them in the United States, and my brother, including my brother. I was very, you know, close. We were very close. Even in the concentration camp, he was my big brother, you know. He always took care of me. And he wanted I should stay here. Anyway, the introduced me to a girl. And the girl was a nice girl. And after a year-- now before when I decided that I-- that I liked the girl. And the girl was to-- wanted to get married, me too. And we decided I'm not going to go back to Israel. We applied for permanent resident in the United States. It took a while, you know, through a lawyer. And he made legal-- legally the papers for me. And I became a legal resident of the United States. I start-- after that, I got a job in plumbing. And I did not bad in plumbing. I succeeded right away. I went right away to school in the United States after being a short time. My brother helped me. And I went to night school. I had to learn the English language. Although before I came, I prepared myself in Israel. For a year I learned the English language. When I came, I knew a little. It helped me tremendously. It helped me a lot when I came to United States, and I knew a little English. After of couple weeks, I started talking English. I spoke English a little, not too well. And I applied for a job. In order to work in plumbing, I had to know well English. I went to school, and since then in the United States I went about close to 11 years night school. I attended various, various courses in, in this. And then I started plumbing, engineering, and I became a licensed plumber, and then a licensed underground tank installer. And I made a nice living in the United States. And I was never sorry that I married an American girl, my wife, Tillie, former Rosenzweig. And I built a nice family. I had two boys, which I was proud of them. And one, Howard, is now a rabbi, Howard Kaminsky. And my other, my older boy, Marvin, is working now in Syracuse. And he, he's doing all right. And I'm proud of them. So that's what it is, and I'm very happy here.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's great.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you been active politically since you came here, since--

speaker: interviewee
text: Politically, they tried again I should be active politically. And I considered. But it wasn't convenient for me. I, I had-- I lived in this, in Bayside. And all the actions were in Manhattan. And for me to work hard in plumbing during the day, and to go to Manhattan was impossible to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what I-- what I did, I became-- after a while, I became president from our society. And for the last 32 years, I was two years vice president. And now it was just 30 years that I am president of the society, of the [INAUDIBLE] society in the United States here. And we are doing fine work. We are helping each other as much as possible in the United States. How many people have belonged to your society?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, we were about 150. But we are getting little, less and less.

speaker: interviewer
text: And are they mostly survivors?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because no more survivors, and only the guys that they survived came about 40 or 50 years ago here. And then we became, I'm sorry to say, we became less and less. Now we are only about 100 families. That's all. And we just met this week. We are meeting every once in a while, especially during the holidays, Purim. We had now the Hanukkah party at [NON-ENGLISH]. And we are all right. We are very happy here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, thank you very much. Is there something you left out that you want to mention? Is there any other-- you gave a very, very thorough story.

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't think of anything else. But I would like just to say in general about, about the Holocaust. During the Holocaust, I saw-- being in the camps, I saw only two types of people, [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] people which were-- which are the killers in the camps, and the ones that they are killed. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] The people that kill us were people. And the ones that they were persecuted was also people. The people-- the killers were Christians. And the persecuted were the Jewish people. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] I, I, I couldn't understand-- it was very hard. For me, I understood, because I suffered one of them-- why the killers were Christians. This, I would like to leave to the Christians themselves. They should find out why the Christians were the killers. For the Jewish people, I would like to find-- they should find out why the Jewish people were the victims. Even I didn't have a clear picture why. Just because a son of Jewish parents, I had to become a victim. This was for many people that they couldn't understand. They were-- what I would like to leave-- the Jewish people should find out why they were the victims. Even the future generation should learn about it, why we suffered so much during the Holocaust. I lost my family. I lost my little brothers and sisters. I lost my parents, lost everything. In order this shouldn't be repeated once in the histories, I would like they should study. That's all. And see to it that the Jewish people should never, but never, be the victims of a Shoah. And we lost six million of these victims. During-- later on I found out that there were not just two types of people, killers and victims. There were many more people. And I would say the majority of the people in Europe in the occupied by the Nazis Europe, they saw exactly. And they knew exactly what happened to the Jewish people. They kept quiet. I call them the silent majority of the people in Europe. They didn't do a thing. Just a very few people, this was the majority. A very minute people, maybe one in a city, two in a whole district, risked their lives, and they saved some Jewish people. These were really something. Well these people deserve, deserve everything. They risked their life in order to save the people.

speaker: interviewer
text: I would say among the silent majority, Franklin Delano Roosevelt's administration had a front place among them. They were silent, too. Why do I hold that against that? Why do I mention it? Because [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] the Roosevelt administration had a chance to say something. And I don't mean to, to just to do something drastically. If they would say just for the Germans, we know what you are doing to the Jewish people over there. Why are you killing innocent people and children, innocent children, innocent people?

speaker: interviewee
text: If he-- you know, the Germans would take this under consideration because they didn't like it's effect, like I told you before. They created a model camp in Theresienstadt to show the world how they are treating the people, the Jewish people. But this was only one spot, not more. The rest were killing, killings. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: If Roosevelt would say, stop the killings, that's all I was asking. Don't do that. If you will continue, you will pay for it dearly, to the Germans. But the administration didn't do a thing. Even before the war, when England agreed to take several thousand children from Germany, Belgium did, and France, and many places. Do you know that the United States didn't take in not even a single child?

speaker: interviewee
text: When a boat came, and they wanted to come into the United States, they ran away. The United States wouldn't let them in. I forgot the name of it. Just on the tip of my tongue. 907 people that the United States didn't agree to let them in even. Later a transport of 500 people, they came from Germany that they saved themselves. They locked them up in Oswego for over a year. Only after the war, they let them free. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: I love the United States. But why didn't the United States, the administration at that time do something to help? You know, when the people in the Warsaw ghetto were fighting, in order to obtain one gun, do you know what kind of trouble they went through in order to have-- to get a gun? The Polish government from England did droppings. They dropped some, some guns and equipment for the underground.

speaker: interviewee
text: But not even once when the people in Warsaw were fighting for their lives-- for six weeks they kept-- they kept fighting. And they asked for some help. No help came from anywhere, not from our Polish friends, neighbors, and not from England. And England knew exactly what happened to the Jewish people over there at that time. Nothing was done. Not even a leaflet. If they would bring-- drop the leaflet to us, the concentration camp, do you know what it would do to the people if they would read that there are some place, some people do care about us. Because nobody cared about us. We were alone. We had no help whatsoever from anybody. It didn't come. And I blame until now why the administration, American administration, didn't do anything to save the Jewish people. Not to save the Jews, at least to warn Hitler he should stop the killings. That's all. Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much.
